title: Troubleshoot User Authorization Issue
description: Step-by-step process to diagnose and resolve a user access or authorization problem.

steps.action: Capture the failed authorization check
steps.reason: SU53 shows exactly which authorization object, field, and value was missing at the time of the last failed check. Run it immediately after the error — it only stores the most recent failure.

steps.action: Review the user's assigned roles and profiles
steps.reason: Confirm which roles are currently assigned to the user and that they are valid for the current date. A role with expired validity grants no access.

steps.action: Open the relevant role and check the authorization data
steps.reason: On the Authorization tab in PFCG, locate the missing authorization object from SU53. Check whether the required field value is maintained and not restricted.

steps.action: Add or correct the authorization and generate the profile
steps.reason: After editing the authorization, you must regenerate the authorization profile — the change has no effect until the profile is rebuilt and saved.

steps.action: Re-assign or refresh the role on the user if needed
steps.reason: Profile regeneration is usually sufficient. If the role was just added, verify the assignment saved correctly and validity dates are set.

steps.action: Ask the user to re-test the failing action
steps.reason: Confirm the fix worked. If SU53 shows a different failure, repeat the process — multiple missing authorizations may need to be resolved in sequence.

title: Create and Set Up a New SAP User
description: Complete process for creating a new SAP user, assigning roles, and verifying access.

steps.action: Create the user record with logon data and personal info
steps.reason: Set username, first/last name, email, and initial password. Choose the correct user type — Dialog for interactive users, Service for technical/batch users, Communication for RFC users.

steps.action: Assign the required roles on the Roles tab
steps.reason: Roles carry the authorization profiles. Assign all roles the user needs with correct validity dates. Always use roles rather than direct profile assignments.

steps.action: Confirm all assigned roles have current authorization profiles
steps.reason: A yellow or red status indicator on a role means the profile is outdated or missing. Regenerate the profile so the authorizations are active.

steps.action: Ask the user to log on and test their key transactions
steps.reason: Have the user attempt the main tasks they need to perform. If they hit authorization errors, use SU53 immediately after each failure to capture the missing object.

steps.action: Review any access failures and adjust roles via PFCG
steps.reason: Use the SU53 output to fine-tune authorizations in PFCG. Repeat until all required access is confirmed working.

title: Investigate Missing Transport Change
description: How to investigate why a transport imported successfully but the expected change is not visible in the target system.

steps.action: Check the transport import log in STMS
steps.reason: Navigate to the target system's import queue and open the import log. Look carefully for warnings — a green status icon does not mean the import was error-free.

steps.action: Verify the correct client and system were targeted
steps.reason: Customizing transports are client-specific. If the transport was imported to the wrong client, the change will not appear in the expected client.

steps.action: Open the transport request and check its object list
steps.reason: Expand the transport and verify the expected object (table entry, program, config) is actually in the request. If it is missing, it was never added to the transport.

steps.action: Check whether another transport overwrote the object
steps.reason: If multiple transports contain the same object, the last one imported wins. Check the import sequence for the object to detect overwrite conflicts.

steps.action: Re-import the transport if necessary
steps.reason: If the import had errors or targeted the wrong client, re-import from the STMS import queue with the correct settings. Check the log again after re-import.

title: Fix an OData Service Not Found Error
description: Diagnose and resolve a 404 or service unavailable error on an SAP Gateway OData service.

steps.action: Search for the service and check its activation status
steps.reason: The most common cause is the service not being activated on the Gateway. Search by service name and confirm it shows as active. If missing, add it from the backend.

steps.action: Verify the ICF service node is active
steps.reason: Even if activated in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, the underlying HTTP service node in SICF must also be active. Navigate to the OData service path and check its status.

steps.action: Clear the backend metadata cache
steps.reason: Stale backend cache causes the Gateway to fail when retrieving service metadata. Run this on the backend system first, before the frontend cache.

steps.action: Clear the Gateway frontend metadata cache
steps.reason: After clearing the backend cache, clear the Gateway hub cache too. Both must be cleared for a complete refresh of the service metadata.

steps.action: Verify the service definition is complete and regenerated
steps.reason: If the service was recently modified, confirm runtime objects were regenerated in SEGW. Without regeneration, model changes are not active in the Gateway.

steps.action: Test the $metadata URL in a browser or Gateway Client
steps.reason: Access /sap/opu/odata/svc/SERVICE_NAME/$metadata to confirm a valid XML response. Use the Gateway Client in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE for a quick test without leaving SAP.

title: Build and Activate a New OData Service
description: End-to-end flow for developing, registering, and testing a new OData service in SAP Gateway.

steps.action: Create or open the OData project and define the data model
steps.reason: Define entity types, properties, and associations that model your data. The data model determines the shape of the OData $metadata and all entity sets.

steps.action: Implement the DPC_EXT class methods for each entity set
steps.reason: The generated Data Provider Class extension (DPC_EXT) must be enhanced with ABAP code for each operation: GET_ENTITY, GET_ENTITYSET, CREATE_ENTITY, etc.

steps.action: Generate runtime objects
steps.reason: Use Generate → Runtime Objects after any model change. This creates the backend registration artifacts. Without this step, the service definition has no runtime effect.

steps.action: Register the service on the Gateway hub
steps.reason: Add the service in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE to expose it on the Gateway URL. Set the system alias to point to the backend where the service was built.

steps.action: Activate the ICF node if not already active
steps.reason: Ensure the HTTP service path for the OData service is active. Without an active ICF node, the Gateway returns HTTP 403 or 404 regardless of service registration.

steps.action: Use the SAP Gateway Client to test $metadata and entity sets
steps.reason: The built-in Gateway Client lets you call service endpoints without leaving SAP. Test $metadata first, then each entity set to confirm correct data is returned.

title: Investigate a Missing or Incorrect FI Document
description: How to find, review, and troubleshoot a financial accounting document that is missing, incorrect, or failed to post.

steps.action: Display the document by number, company code, and fiscal year
steps.reason: If you have the document number, start here to see exactly what was posted — header data, line items, G/L accounts, and posting keys.

steps.action: Search for the document via G/L account line items if number is unknown
steps.reason: FBL3N lets you filter by account, date, amount, and reference. Use it when you need to find a document without knowing its number.

steps.action: Check the G/L account balance to confirm whether the posting landed
steps.reason: If a posting should have hit a specific G/L account, verify the balance in FS10N. Period totals help confirm whether the document was included in the period.

steps.action: Check if the posting period is open for the document date
steps.reason: A closed posting period is one of the most common causes of posting failures. Ask an FI admin to check period settings in OB52 if the period appears closed.

steps.action: Check the system log if the document appears to have failed silently
steps.reason: Update task failures can cause documents to appear posted in the UI but not actually written to the database. SM21 will show update failures for the relevant timeframe.

title: Diagnose a Failed or Empty Payment Run
description: Step-by-step approach for investigating why the F110 automatic payment run did not select expected vendor invoices or produced no payments.

steps.action: Open the payment run and review the parameters
steps.reason: Check the company code, payment methods, vendor range, and next payment date. A single wrong parameter can exclude all expected vendors.

steps.action: Review the payment proposal log for exclusion reasons
steps.reason: The payment proposal displays each item considered and the reason it was excluded (payment block, no payment method, past due date, etc.).

steps.action: Check the vendor's open items directly
steps.reason: Confirm the invoice is open (not already cleared) and has no payment block indicator. FBL1N shows all open items with their attributes at a glance.

steps.action: Display the invoice and check the payment method and terms
steps.reason: The payment method on the invoice must match the payment run configuration. Also check that the due date falls within the 'Next payment date' window in F110.

steps.action: Remove any payment blocks and re-run the proposal
steps.reason: Once you have identified the exclusion reason, correct it (remove block, fix payment method, open period) and re-run the payment proposal to confirm selection.

title: Investigate a Blocked Sales Order
description: How to find and resolve delivery or billing blocks on a sales order that is stuck in processing.

steps.action: Display the sales order and check header-level blocks
steps.reason: On the Sales tab of the order header, look for Delivery Block and Billing Block fields. Note the block reason — this tells you the category of the problem.

steps.action: Check the Status Overview tab for the full document flow
steps.reason: The status overview shows whether a delivery or invoice was already created, and where the process is stuck. This prevents duplicate actions.

steps.action: If credit-blocked, contact the credit manager for release
steps.reason: Credit blocks are set automatically when the customer exceeds their credit limit. Only a credit manager should release these — do not remove them without authorization.

steps.action: Remove the delivery or billing block if authorized
steps.reason: In VA02, navigate to the header Sales tab and clear the block field. Save the order. The delivery or billing run can now pick up the order.

steps.action: Verify the block is removed and the order status updated
steps.reason: Re-display VA03 to confirm the block is gone and the order status allows further processing. Trigger delivery creation if needed.

title: Process and Verify a Goods Receipt
description: End-to-end steps for posting a goods receipt against a purchase order and verifying stock is updated correctly.

steps.action: Display the purchase order and confirm the expected delivery
steps.reason: Before posting the GR, verify the PO is open, quantities are correct, and no full GR has already been posted against the relevant line items.

steps.action: Post the goods receipt (movement type 101) referencing the PO number
steps.reason: In MIGO, select 'Goods Receipt' and 'Purchase Order'. Enter the PO number to load the expected items. Confirm quantities and storage location before posting.

steps.action: Note the material document number after posting
steps.reason: MIGO generates a material document number on successful posting. Record this for reference — it is needed for reversals or further investigation.

steps.action: Verify stock levels updated correctly
steps.reason: After posting, open MMBE for the material and plant to confirm unrestricted stock increased by the received quantity.

steps.action: Check the PO delivery status
steps.reason: Re-display the PO and check the Delivery Status on the item — it should now show as fully or partially delivered depending on the quantity received.

title: Diagnose an RFC Connection Failure
description: Step-by-step approach to diagnosing and resolving a failing RFC destination between SAP systems.

steps.action: Open the RFC destination and run the Connection Test
steps.reason: SM59 shows all configured RFC destinations. The 'Test Connection' button confirms whether the network layer is reachable. A failure here points to a network or hostname issue.

steps.action: Run Remote Login to test application-layer connectivity
steps.reason: If the connection test passes but Remote Login fails, the issue is with the logon credentials or the target system's availability — not the network.

steps.action: Verify the connection parameters: host, system number, client, and credentials
steps.reason: Even a small typo in the hostname or system number will prevent the connection. Confirm these values against the target system's instance profile.

steps.action: Check the system log on both sides for RFC errors
steps.reason: SM21 on the calling and target systems often reveals the root cause — authentication failures, authorization issues, or system-level connectivity errors.

steps.action: Confirm the target system RFC user has the correct authorizations
steps.reason: RFC technical users need authorization object S_RFC. Check SU53 on the target system if the connection test produces an authorization error.

steps.action: Save updated settings and re-test
steps.reason: After correcting parameters or permissions, save the RFC destination and run both tests again to confirm end-to-end connectivity is restored.

title: Investigate a Failed IDoc
description: Step-by-step approach for analyzing and reprocessing an inbound or outbound IDoc in error.

steps.action: Find the IDoc by status, message type, partner, or date
steps.reason: WE02 is the best starting point when you know an interface failed but do not yet know the exact root cause.

steps.action: Open the IDoc and review the current status record and error text
steps.reason: The status records usually show whether the problem is posting logic, master data, partner profile setup, or an application error.

steps.action: Refine the search if needed by message type or partner profile
steps.reason: WE05 is useful when you need broader filtering across many IDocs or want to confirm whether the issue affects multiple messages.

steps.action: Fix the underlying cause before retrying
steps.reason: Do not reprocess until the real issue is resolved, otherwise the IDoc will simply fail again with the same status.

steps.action: Reprocess the failed IDoc
steps.reason: BD87 is the standard transaction for retrying failed IDocs after the root cause has been corrected.

steps.action: Confirm the IDoc reached a successful status
steps.reason: Always verify that the final status is successful and that any downstream business document was actually created.

title: Investigate a Purchase Order Not Released
description: How to diagnose and resolve a purchase order that is waiting for approval or not progressing through release strategy.

steps.action: Display the purchase order and review the current release status
steps.reason: Start by checking whether the PO is actually subject to release strategy and which release code or status is pending.

steps.action: Check whether the document was changed after an earlier release
steps.reason: Certain changes reset the release status and force the approval process to start again.

steps.action: Confirm the responsible approver or release code is correct
steps.reason: If the strategy determination is wrong, the issue is often with document values, organizational data, or release configuration.

steps.action: Release the purchase order if you have the correct authorization
steps.reason: ME29N is the standard transaction for approving a PO in a release strategy process.

steps.action: Re-display the PO and confirm the release status updated
steps.reason: Always confirm the PO is now released and available for follow-on processing.

title: Investigate a Blocked Vendor Invoice
description: Step-by-step process for analyzing and releasing a blocked invoice after quantity or price variances are resolved.

steps.action: Locate the vendor invoice and confirm its current status
steps.reason: Start by confirming the invoice is open and identify whether it is blocked for payment or blocked due to invoice verification variances.

steps.action: Review the invoice details and compare against the PO and goods receipt
steps.reason: Most blocked invoices come from quantity or price mismatches between the invoice, PO, and GR.

steps.action: Correct the underlying variance if needed
steps.reason: Do not release the invoice until the business reason for the block has been resolved.

steps.action: Release the blocked invoice
steps.reason: MRBR is the standard transaction for releasing invoices blocked by invoice verification rules.

steps.action: Confirm the invoice is now eligible for payment
steps.reason: Recheck the vendor line item to ensure the block is gone and the invoice can be picked up by payment processing.

title: Investigate a Missing Workflow Item in Inbox
description: How to diagnose why an approval or workitem is not appearing in the user's SAP inbox.

steps.action: Check whether the workitem is already present in the user's inbox
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is simply that the user is checking the wrong folder or has not refreshed the inbox.

steps.action: Search for the workflow workitem by task, user, or status
steps.reason: SWI5 helps determine whether the workitem exists and whether it is assigned, completed, or stuck.

steps.action: Verify whether an agent was determined for the workitem
steps.reason: If no agent is assigned, the workflow cannot appear in anyone's inbox.

steps.action: Use administrative processing if the workflow must be unblocked
steps.reason: SWIA allows an administrator to forward, execute, or complete a stuck workitem when normal processing is not possible.

steps.action: Ask the user to refresh the inbox and confirm the item is visible
steps.reason: Once the assignment or workflow issue is fixed, the user should verify that the item is now available for action.

title: Investigate Data That Did Not Save
description: How to diagnose a transaction that looked successful but did not actually commit data to the database.

steps.action: Search for failed update requests around the time of the issue
steps.reason: SM13 is the primary place to confirm whether the update task failed after the user saved the transaction.

steps.action: Open the failed update and review the error details
steps.reason: The update record often points directly to the program, function module, or business object that failed.

steps.action: Check for a related short dump
steps.reason: Many failed update requests also generate a dump, which gives more detailed technical information.

steps.action: Review the system log around the same timestamp
steps.reason: SM21 may show update terminations, enqueue issues, or system-level errors not obvious from the business transaction.

steps.action: Fix the root cause and repeat the business transaction if appropriate
steps.reason: Once the technical or data issue is resolved, the original business action may need to be entered again.

title: Investigate Missing or Stuck Spool Output
description: How to diagnose why a report, form, or print output did not appear or did not print as expected.

steps.action: Confirm the background job or report finished successfully
steps.reason: If the generating program failed, there may be no spool request at all. Start by confirming the source process completed.

steps.action: Search for the spool request by user, date, or program
steps.reason: SP01 is the main place to verify whether output was generated and which spool request number belongs to it.

steps.action: Open the spool request and check its status and output device
steps.reason: The spool may exist but be tied to the wrong printer, be incomplete, or remain in a waiting status.

steps.action: Verify the target printer and user defaults
steps.reason: A common cause is output going to a different printer or logical output device than expected.

steps.action: Reprint or forward the spool request if needed
steps.reason: Once the cause is identified, you can often resend the existing spool without rerunning the business process.

title: Investigate a Record Locked by Another User or Process
description: How to diagnose and safely resolve SAP lock entry issues when a record or object is stuck locked.

steps.action: Search for the lock entry by user, table, or object key
steps.reason: SM12 shows which user or process currently holds the lock and what object is affected.

steps.action: Check whether the locking session is still active
steps.reason: If the user is genuinely editing the object, deleting the lock can cause inconsistent processing.

steps.action: Review the system log if the lock appears orphaned
steps.reason: System or communication errors can leave behind locks after a failed update or abnormal termination.

steps.action: Ask the user or owning team to exit the transaction cleanly if possible
steps.reason: The safest resolution is always to let the owning session release the lock normally.

steps.action: Delete the stale lock only if you are sure it is no longer in use
steps.reason: Manual lock deletion should be the last step and only after confirming the original session is gone.

title: Investigate a Stuck qRFC Queue
description: How to diagnose why queued RFC messages are not moving through outbound or inbound processing.

steps.action: Check the outbound queue in the source system
steps.reason: If the message never leaves the source system, the problem starts in SMQ1 with outbound processing.

steps.action: Check the inbound queue in the target system
steps.reason: If the message arrives but does not process, SMQ2 shows the stuck inbound queue and its current error text.

steps.action: Review the application log for the related interface or object
steps.reason: SLG1 often contains the clearest business-level explanation for why a queued process failed.

steps.action: Verify the RFC destination if communication is failing
steps.reason: Queue issues are often caused by RFC destinations that are down, misconfigured, or not authorized.

steps.action: Fix the root cause before restarting the queue
steps.reason: Restarting without correction usually results in the queue failing again immediately.

steps.action: Restart or unlock the affected queue
steps.reason: After the issue is fixed, restart processing and confirm the queued messages are cleared successfully.

title: Investigate a Purchase Requisition Waiting for Release
description: How to diagnose and resolve a purchase requisition that is waiting for approval or stuck in release strategy.

steps.action: Display the purchase requisition and review its release status
steps.reason: Start by checking whether the requisition is actually subject to release strategy and what approval step is pending.

steps.action: Review document details that influence release determination
steps.reason: Values such as plant, purchasing group, account assignment, and total value often determine the release strategy.

steps.action: Confirm the responsible approver and release strategy logic
steps.reason: If the wrong strategy was triggered, the issue is usually in master data, document content, or configuration.

steps.action: Release the purchase requisition if authorized
steps.reason: ME54N is the standard transaction for approving PRs under a release strategy.

steps.action: Re-display the requisition and confirm the updated release status
steps.reason: Always verify the requisition is now approved and available for follow-on processing.

title: Investigate Pricing Not Determined on a Sales Order
description: How to diagnose why a price, discount, or condition is missing or incorrect on a sales order.

steps.action: Open the sales order and review the pricing conditions on the item
steps.reason: Start by confirming exactly which condition type is missing or incorrect and whether pricing was carried out at all.

steps.action: Check the sales area, customer, material, and pricing date
steps.reason: Pricing depends heavily on organizational data and validity dates. A mismatch here often explains missing conditions.

steps.action: Display the expected condition record
steps.reason: VK13 shows whether a valid condition record exists for the relevant key combination and date.

steps.action: Verify whether the pricing procedure and condition determination are correct
steps.reason: If the record exists but is not found, the issue may be in pricing procedure configuration or the access sequence.

steps.action: Re-price the order after correcting the underlying issue
steps.reason: Once the record or configuration problem is fixed, re-run pricing so the sales order reflects the correct values.

title: Investigate Why an Outbound Delivery Was Not Created
description: How to diagnose why a sales order is not resulting in an outbound delivery or why delivery processing is blocked.

steps.action: Display the sales order and review overall status and item status
steps.reason: The order may still be blocked, incomplete, or not yet due for delivery.

steps.action: Check delivery-relevant dates, quantities, and any blocks
steps.reason: Delivery creation depends on schedule lines, confirmed quantity, shipping point determination, and absence of delivery blocks.

steps.action: Attempt delivery creation manually if appropriate
steps.reason: Manual creation helps confirm whether the order is actually delivery-relevant and shows immediate error messages if not.

steps.action: Check whether a delivery already exists
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is not missing creation, but that the delivery exists and is stuck later in the process.

steps.action: Review and complete outstanding delivery processing steps
steps.reason: If the delivery exists, VL02N is used for picking, packing, and posting goods issue.

title: Investigate a Closed FI Posting Period
description: How to diagnose and resolve a posting that fails because the accounting period is not open.

steps.action: Capture the exact posting date, company code, and account type from the error
steps.reason: Those values determine which period control entry is relevant and whether the posting should be allowed.

steps.action: Check whether the posting period is open for the relevant account type
steps.reason: OB52 is the standard place to confirm whether FI posting periods are open or closed.

steps.action: Verify whether the user is posting in the intended fiscal period
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is simply that the posting date is wrong or falls into a locked prior period.

steps.action: Open the period if you are authorized and the business allows it
steps.reason: Period opening is a controlled accounting activity and should only be done with the right approval.

steps.action: Repeat the posting after the period issue is resolved
steps.reason: Once the posting window is valid, the original document can be entered again.

title: Investigate Missing or Incorrect Leave Quota
description: How to diagnose why an employee has no leave balance, too little quota, or a missing accrual.

steps.action: Check the employee's current quota balances
steps.reason: Start by confirming whether the quota truly is missing, exhausted, or simply lower than expected.

steps.action: Review or rerun time evaluation if accrual should have happened
steps.reason: Quota accrual is often generated in time evaluation, so missing accruals usually point back to PT60.

steps.action: Review quota-relevant employee master data
steps.reason: Work schedule, employment status, and HR master data can all affect accrual and eligibility.

steps.action: Adjust or correct the quota if a manual fix is required
steps.reason: Infotype 2006 is where quota balances are maintained when correction is necessary.

steps.action: Recheck the employee's balance after correction
steps.reason: Always confirm the quota is now correct before asking the employee to retry the leave process.

title: Hire and Place a New Employee in the Organization
description: Step-by-step process for creating a new employee, maintaining core HR data, and verifying organizational assignment.

steps.action: Execute the hiring action for the new employee
steps.reason: PA40 is the standard starting point because it chains the key HR infotype steps needed for a new hire.

steps.action: Review and complete the required employee master data infotypes
steps.reason: Personal data, organizational assignment, payroll, and time-related infotypes must be complete for the employee to function correctly in downstream processes.

steps.action: Check the organizational unit and position assignment
steps.reason: If the employee is not correctly placed in OM, workflows, approvals, and reporting structures may not work as expected.

steps.action: Display the org structure and confirm the employee appears in the right place
steps.reason: A read-only org display is a quick way to validate the final organizational placement from a business perspective.

steps.action: Perform a final read-only validation of the employee master data
steps.reason: PA20 is useful as a clean final check to confirm the record is complete and visible correctly.

title: Investigate a Slow Transaction or General SAP Performance Issue
description: How to diagnose whether slowness comes from workload, a specific user action, database access, or a busy application server.

steps.action: Review workload and response time trends
steps.reason: Start broad to confirm whether the issue is isolated to one transaction or part of a larger system performance pattern.

steps.action: Check active work processes on the affected server
steps.reason: Hung or long-running work processes can explain why one or more users experience major slowness.

steps.action: Review work processes across all servers
steps.reason: If the issue is system-wide, the global work process view gives a better picture than a single-instance view.

steps.action: Confirm the affected application server landscape
steps.reason: This helps determine whether the user is tied to a specific problematic instance or whether the issue spans the full system.

steps.action: Run a focused trace for the affected transaction or user
steps.reason: Use ST05 when you need detailed evidence of expensive SQL, RFC, or other low-level performance bottlenecks.

title: Investigate an Active OData Service Returning a Runtime Error
description: How to diagnose an OData service that is active and reachable but fails at runtime with a backend exception or HTTP 500-style error.

steps.action: Find the failed request in the Gateway error log
steps.reason: This is the best starting point when the service is active but the request itself is failing during processing.

steps.action: Open the technical details and backend exception text
steps.reason: The error log often points directly to the failing entity set, method, or backend exception.

steps.action: Review the service implementation and relevant runtime objects
steps.reason: If the issue comes from a model mismatch or provider logic, SEGW is the right place to validate service design and generation.

steps.action: Check for a short dump at the same timestamp
steps.reason: Backend OData runtime failures often generate an ABAP short dump that contains the real root cause.

steps.action: Retest the service after fixing the backend issue
steps.reason: Once the exception is resolved, use the Gateway client or service test to confirm the request now succeeds.

title: Investigate an SAP Email Not Sent
description: How to diagnose outbound emails that are waiting, failing, or never leaving SAP.

steps.action: Locate the send request and review its status
steps.reason: SOST shows whether the message is waiting, failed, or already handed over for sending.

steps.action: Open the message details and error text
steps.reason: The send request details often show whether the issue is recipient-related, node-related, or scheduling-related.

steps.action: Check SAPconnect node configuration and send scheduling
steps.reason: If many messages are stuck, the problem is often in SMTP configuration or the send job setup.

steps.action: Confirm the recipient address and business trigger are correct
steps.reason: Sometimes the system worked correctly but the mail was addressed incorrectly or never generated as expected.

steps.action: Retry or monitor the send request after correction
steps.reason: Once configuration or data is fixed, confirm the mail leaves SAP successfully.

title: Investigate a Workflow That Failed with an Error
description: How to diagnose a workflow instance that started but encountered an execution error or stopped at a specific step.

steps.action: Locate the workflow instance and open its technical log
steps.reason: SWI1 gives the clearest end-to-end view of where the workflow stopped and which step failed.

steps.action: Review related workitems by status or task
steps.reason: This helps confirm whether the issue is isolated to one item or affects multiple instances.

steps.action: Identify whether the problem is agent assignment, business data, or a technical exception
steps.reason: The remediation path depends on whether the workflow failed in routing, business logic, or execution.

steps.action: Administratively process or forward the workitem if needed
steps.reason: SWIA is useful once you understand the cause and need to unblock or complete the stuck item.

steps.action: Confirm the user-facing inbox result after the fix
steps.reason: After resolving the issue, validate that the workflow now reaches the expected user or completes correctly.

title: Investigate a Sales Order Credit Block
description: How to diagnose and release a sales order blocked by credit management.

steps.action: Display the sales order and confirm it is blocked by credit
steps.reason: Start by verifying that the actual blocking reason is credit and not delivery, billing, or incompletion.

steps.action: Review customer credit situation and business reason for the block
steps.reason: Credit blocks are usually triggered by exceeded limits, overdue receivables, or risk controls.

steps.action: Review the blocked sales document in credit management
steps.reason: VKM3 is the standard place to review and release credit-blocked sales orders.

steps.action: Release the order if business approval exists
steps.reason: Only release the document if the block is understood and approval to proceed exists.

steps.action: Re-display the order and confirm the credit block is gone
steps.reason: Always confirm the document is now available for follow-on processing.

title: Investigate an MM Posting Blocked by Period Issue
description: How to diagnose inventory or materials postings that fail because the MM period is not open.

steps.action: Capture the exact posting date, plant, and material movement being attempted
steps.reason: You need the specific context to determine whether the issue is date-related, plant-related, or process-related.

steps.action: Check the current MM period status
steps.reason: MMPV controls MM period closing and opening, which directly affects whether stock postings are allowed.

steps.action: Confirm whether the posting date belongs in the intended MM period
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is not the period setup, but that the posting date is incorrect for the business event.

steps.action: Advance the MM period if authorized and appropriate
steps.reason: Period changes should only be performed with proper controls, but this is the standard correction when the next period is needed.

steps.action: Retry the goods movement after period correction
steps.reason: Once the period is open, the original material posting can be executed again.

title: Reverse an Incorrect FI Document
description: How to identify, reverse, and validate the cancellation of a wrongly posted accounting document.

steps.action: Display the posted document and confirm it is the one to reverse
steps.reason: Always verify the company code, fiscal year, amounts, and line items before attempting reversal.

steps.action: Check whether the document is eligible for reversal
steps.reason: Some postings may already be cleared, related to follow-on documents, or subject to period restrictions.

steps.action: Reverse the accounting document
steps.reason: FB08 is the standard transaction for canceling an FI document and creating the reversal entry.

steps.action: Display both original and reversal documents
steps.reason: Confirm the reversal document was created correctly and that the original is marked as reversed.

steps.action: Check account balances if needed
steps.reason: For material accounting or reconciliation concerns, verify the expected balances after reversal.

title: Post a Manual Vendor Payment
description: Step-by-step process for posting a manual outgoing payment and clearing vendor open items.

steps.action: Review the vendor's open items and identify what should be paid
steps.reason: Start by confirming the invoice is open, due, and ready to be cleared.

steps.action: Display the invoice if you need to validate payment terms or details
steps.reason: This helps confirm you are clearing the correct item and understand any references or terms.

steps.action: Post the outgoing payment and clear the open item
steps.reason: F-53 is the standard transaction for a manual vendor payment with open item clearing.

steps.action: Confirm the item is now cleared
steps.reason: Recheck vendor line items to ensure the posting cleared the intended invoice.

title: Post a Manual Customer Payment
description: How to post an incoming customer payment and clear open receivables manually.

steps.action: Review the customer's open receivables
steps.reason: Start by confirming which invoice or invoices should be cleared by the incoming payment.

steps.action: Display the invoice if additional validation is needed
steps.reason: This helps confirm the customer reference, amount, and due date before posting.

steps.action: Post the incoming payment and clear the open items
steps.reason: F-28 is the standard transaction for manual incoming customer payments and receivable clearing.

steps.action: Confirm the receivable is now cleared
steps.reason: Recheck the customer line items to verify the payment cleared the intended invoice.

title: Investigate a Timesheet Waiting for Approval
description: How to diagnose why time data was entered but not yet approved or transferred downstream.

steps.action: Check the status of the employee's time entry
steps.reason: The timesheet may be saved but still waiting in an unapproved or in-process state.

steps.action: Review pending time approvals
steps.reason: CAT7 is the standard place to approve submitted working times in CATS-based processes.

steps.action: Confirm the right approver or approval process is being used
steps.reason: If entries never appear for approval, the issue may be in workflow routing or organizational setup.

steps.action: Approve the submitted time if appropriate
steps.reason: Once approved, downstream transfer or visibility issues can be retested.

steps.action: Confirm the approved time is visible in downstream HR balances if relevant
steps.reason: This final check verifies that approval translated into the expected business effect.

title: Correct Employee Time Data
description: How to adjust employee time infotypes when attendances, absences, or time records are incorrect.

steps.action: Open the relevant employee and time infotype
steps.reason: PA61 is used specifically for time-related infotypes and corrections.

steps.action: Identify the incorrect absence or attendance record
steps.reason: Before changing data, confirm the exact date, subtype, and business reason for the correction.

steps.action: Change or correct the time data record
steps.reason: Use the proper infotype maintenance process to keep HR time data consistent.

steps.action: Review downstream quota or absence balances if relevant
steps.reason: Time corrections can affect leave balances and related HR calculations.

steps.action: Rerun time evaluation if the correction requires recalculation
steps.reason: Some corrections only take full effect after time evaluation processes the updated data.

title: Investigate a Stuck or Failed tRFC Call
description: How to diagnose transactional RFC calls that are waiting, failing repeatedly, or not reaching the target system.

steps.action: Find the failed or waiting tRFC entry by date, user, or destination
steps.reason: SM58 is the standard place to see whether a transactional RFC call is waiting for retry, failed with an error, or blocked by communication issues.

steps.action: Open the entry and review the exact error text and destination
steps.reason: The stored error usually tells you whether the issue is communication, logon, authorization, or an application problem in the target system.

steps.action: Test the RFC destination used by the failed call
steps.reason: If the connection itself is broken, the tRFC entry will keep failing until the destination, credentials, or network path is corrected.

steps.action: Check the system log around the same timestamp
steps.reason: SM21 often shows supporting technical information such as communication failures, logon problems, or system-side outages.

steps.action: Fix the underlying destination, authorization, or target-system issue
steps.reason: Do not just retry the entry blindly — the real cause must be resolved first, otherwise the call will fail again.

steps.action: Repeat or reprocess the tRFC entry after correction
steps.reason: Once the cause is fixed, reprocessing confirms whether the call now reaches the target system successfully.

title: Trace an Authorization Failure When SU53 Is Not Enough
description: How to capture a deeper authorization trace for access issues that cannot be explained clearly by SU53 alone.

steps.action: Capture the most recent failed authorization check first
steps.reason: SU53 is still the fastest first check and may already reveal the missing object or field value without needing a full trace.

steps.action: Activate an authorization trace for the affected user
steps.reason: ST01 records every authorization check during the failing action, including checks that SU53 may not show clearly.

steps.action: Ask the user to repeat the exact failing step while the trace is active
steps.reason: The trace is only useful if it captures the real business action that is currently failing.

steps.action: Stop the trace and analyze the failed authorization checks
steps.reason: Review which authorization objects were checked, which values were requested, and which check returned a failure.

steps.action: Adjust the relevant role authorization values if needed
steps.reason: Once the exact missing object and value are proven, update the role correctly instead of guessing.

steps.action: Have the user test again after the role correction
steps.reason: A retest confirms whether the traced authorization issue is fully resolved or whether an additional check still fails.

title: Analyze a Slow Custom Transaction or Program
description: How to investigate a transaction or report that runs slowly and identify the expensive ABAP processing steps.

steps.action: Set up a runtime analysis for the affected user, transaction, or program
steps.reason: SAT can trace a specific execution and show where the system spends time during the actual slow step.

steps.action: Execute the slow business action during the measurement
steps.reason: The analysis only becomes useful if it captures the exact runtime path that users are experiencing as slow.

steps.action: Review the call hierarchy, processing times, and expensive statements
steps.reason: This shows whether the slowness comes from custom ABAP logic, repeated calls, database access, or external function modules.

steps.action: Use SQL trace as a follow-up if database access looks expensive
steps.reason: If SAT shows heavy database time, ST05 gives the detailed SQL-level evidence needed for optimization.

steps.action: Identify whether the issue is code logic, data volume, or database access
steps.reason: The right fix depends on the root cause, so separate slow ABAP loops from slow SQL or unusually large data sets.

steps.action: Retest after optimization or correction
steps.reason: A fresh runtime measurement confirms whether the implemented change actually improved performance.

title: Investigate a Table Maintenance Issue
description: How to diagnose why a configuration table cannot be maintained through SM30 or why the expected table view does not work.

steps.action: Enter the table or view name and check whether a maintenance dialog exists
steps.reason: Not every table is maintainable through SM30. Some objects have no generated maintenance view or are meant to be maintained elsewhere.

steps.action: Review the exact error or restriction message
steps.reason: The message usually tells you whether the issue is missing authorization, no maintenance object, or client-dependent restrictions.

steps.action: Display the table contents to confirm the target entries and keys
steps.reason: SE16N helps verify whether the table contains the expected data and whether you are looking at the correct client and key values.

steps.action: Check whether the data should be maintained through IMG instead of SM30
steps.reason: Many configuration objects are intended to be maintained through a controlled customizing activity rather than direct table maintenance.

steps.action: Confirm the user has the right authorization and the correct maintenance path
steps.reason: Even with a valid maintenance view, missing table maintenance authorization or the wrong process path can block the change.

title: Investigate Why a Document Is Missing from the Billing Due List
description: How to diagnose why a sales or delivery document is not appearing in the billing due list and therefore cannot be invoiced as expected.

steps.action: Search the billing due list with the relevant sales or delivery selection criteria
steps.reason: Start by confirming whether the document is truly absent or simply filtered out by billing type, date, organizational data, or user selection.

steps.action: Check whether the delivery is complete and goods issue has been posted
steps.reason: Many billing processes depend on a completed outbound delivery with the right status before the document becomes billing-relevant.

steps.action: Review the sales order billing status and billing block fields
steps.reason: A billing block or incomplete billing-relevant status at header or item level prevents the document from appearing in VF04.

steps.action: Confirm that the document is actually billing-relevant for the intended process
steps.reason: The issue may be configuration or document type related rather than an operational problem in billing execution.

steps.action: Attempt manual billing creation if appropriate
steps.reason: A manual attempt can reveal the direct blocking error and helps confirm whether the issue is in selection logic or document readiness.

title: Investigate an SSL Certificate or Trust Problem
description: How to diagnose secure connection failures caused by missing, expired, or untrusted certificates in SAP.

steps.action: Identify the relevant trust store and review the current certificate chain
steps.reason: You first need to confirm which SSL client or server PSE is involved and whether the expected certificates are present.

steps.action: Check whether the certificate is expired, missing, or not trusted
steps.reason: Most SSL issues come from expiry, missing intermediate certificates, or a certificate chain that SAP does not trust.

steps.action: Retest the secure destination if the issue affects an RFC or HTTP destination
steps.reason: A test after reviewing trust settings helps confirm whether the handshake failure is tied to the certificate problem you found.

steps.action: Check the secure service configuration if the issue affects an SAP-hosted HTTPS service
steps.reason: For inbound or service-side issues, SICF helps verify the affected service path and related security setup.

steps.action: Import or replace the required certificate chain if needed
steps.reason: The trust issue is only resolved once the correct root, intermediate, or server certificate is available and trusted by SAP.

title: Investigate Missing Automatic Account Determination
description: How to diagnose MM-related postings that fail because SAP cannot determine the correct G/L accounts automatically.

steps.action: Capture the exact posting scenario, movement type, plant, and material involved
steps.reason: Automatic account determination depends on the business context, so you need the precise posting situation before checking configuration.

steps.action: Review the material valuation class and relevant master data
steps.reason: The valuation class is one of the main keys used in OBYC to determine which G/L account SAP should post to.

steps.action: Check the relevant transaction key and account determination entry
steps.reason: OBYC is the standard place to verify whether the combination of valuation grouping code, valuation class, and transaction key is maintained.

steps.action: Confirm the posting is using the correct valuation area and configuration context
steps.reason: An account entry may exist in OBYC but still not match if the plant, valuation grouping, or material setup is different from what you expect.

steps.action: Repeat the posting after correcting the account determination setup
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms that SAP can now derive the intended G/L account automatically.

title: Investigate Missing Output Determination
description: How to diagnose why a sales, billing, or purchasing document did not generate the expected output message such as print, email, or EDI.

steps.action: Open the affected document and check whether any output record exists
steps.reason: This confirms whether output was determined incorrectly or not determined at all.

steps.action: Review the output type and access sequence configuration
steps.reason: Output determination depends on the application, output type, access sequence, and requirement routine.

steps.action: Check whether a valid condition record exists for the output type
steps.reason: Without a matching condition record, SAP cannot determine the message automatically.

steps.action: Verify partner, language, communication data, and document context
steps.reason: The output record may exist but fail to match because the document data differs from what the condition record expects.

steps.action: Retest or redetermine output after correcting configuration or condition records
steps.reason: Once the missing setup is fixed, a retest confirms that message determination now works.

title: Resolve a Blocking Incompletion Log
description: How to identify and fix missing required fields that block delivery, billing, or further processing.

steps.action: Open the document and display the incompletion log
steps.reason: The incompletion log shows exactly which required fields are missing.

steps.action: Identify whether the missing field belongs to header, item, partner, or master data
steps.reason: The source of the missing data determines where the fix should be made.

steps.action: Maintain the missing document data if it can be corrected directly
steps.reason: Some incompletion issues are document-specific and can be fixed immediately.

steps.action: Correct the relevant master data if the missing value should come automatically
steps.reason: Recurring incompletion problems often come from incomplete customer, material, or partner master data.

steps.action: Recheck the incompletion log and downstream status
steps.reason: Confirm that the document is now complete and can move to the next process step.

title: Investigate Missing Confirmed Quantity
description: How to diagnose why availability check is not confirming stock for a sales order item.

steps.action: Review the sales order item and confirm the requested versus confirmed quantity
steps.reason: This verifies that the problem is really in availability confirmation and not another sales block.

steps.action: Check the ATP situation for the material and plant
steps.reason: CO09 shows available stock, receipts, requirements, and how SAP is calculating confirmation.

steps.action: Check current stock balances
steps.reason: You need to confirm whether the system truly has unrestricted stock available.

steps.action: Review planned receipts and requirements
steps.reason: MD04 helps explain whether planned supply or competing demand is consuming the stock.

steps.action: Retest the confirmation after correcting stock, dates, or planning data
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms that ATP can now allocate the quantity.

title: Investigate Failed Batch Determination
description: How to diagnose why SAP could not automatically find a valid batch during delivery, goods movement, or order processing.

steps.action: Open the affected document and identify where batch determination was expected
steps.reason: The business context determines which batch search strategy and stock conditions apply.

steps.action: Check whether valid batches exist for the material
steps.reason: No strategy can succeed if there are no eligible batches in stock.

steps.action: Review stock by batch and storage location
steps.reason: Batch stock availability is often the real reason SAP did not propose a batch.

steps.action: Review the batch search strategy and condition records
steps.reason: Automatic batch determination depends on valid strategy setup and matching condition records.

steps.action: Correct classification, stock, or strategy setup and retest
steps.reason: The issue is resolved only when a valid batch is actually proposed or accepted.

title: Investigate Missing Source of Supply
description: How to diagnose why SAP cannot determine a valid vendor or source for a purchase requisition or material.

steps.action: Review the requisition material, plant, and purchasing context
steps.reason: Source determination depends on the exact material, plant, and procurement scenario.

steps.action: Check whether a valid source list entry exists
steps.reason: A source list often controls which vendors are allowed or fixed for a material.

steps.action: Review quota arrangement if used
steps.reason: Quota arrangement may control source selection when multiple valid sources exist.

steps.action: Confirm there is a valid purchasing info record where required
steps.reason: Missing purchasing info records can prevent automatic sourcing and pricing.

steps.action: Retest source assignment or vendor selection after correcting master data
steps.reason: A valid retest confirms the source can now be determined.

title: Create or Correct a Purchasing Info Record
description: How to diagnose missing vendor-material purchasing data and maintain the correct info record.

steps.action: Check whether an info record exists for the vendor and material
steps.reason: This confirms whether the issue is a missing record or incomplete data in an existing record.

steps.action: Review purchasing organization, plant, prices, and validity data
steps.reason: Incorrect org-level data can make an otherwise existing info record unusable.

steps.action: Correct the existing info record if the record exists but is incomplete
steps.reason: Most issues are caused by missing org data, outdated pricing, or wrong validity ranges.

steps.action: Create the info record if none exists
steps.reason: If the record is missing entirely, you need to create it before source or pricing logic can work.

steps.action: Retest the purchasing document after correction
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms the purchasing document can now use the expected vendor-material data.

title: Investigate Missing Vendor Confirmation
description: How to diagnose why a purchase order item has no vendor confirmation or order acknowledgment.

steps.action: Check the PO item for confirmation control and confirmation status
steps.reason: You first need to confirm whether the item is expected to receive a vendor confirmation at all.

steps.action: Review whether a confirmation record exists and is valid
steps.reason: The PO may have confirmation control but still lack actual confirmation data.

steps.action: Check whether document changes or master data affect confirmation expectations
steps.reason: PO changes can reset or invalidate expected confirmation handling.

steps.action: Determine whether the confirmation should arrive manually, by EDI, or through another integration
steps.reason: The resolution depends on how the business process is designed.

steps.action: Validate the updated confirmation status after the fix
steps.reason: The final check confirms the confirmation is now visible and usable.

title: Investigate Missing or Invalid Account Assignment
description: How to diagnose purchasing or logistics documents that fail because cost center, order, WBS, or other assignment data is missing or invalid.

steps.action: Identify the document item and account assignment category involved
steps.reason: The category determines which receiving object is mandatory and how SAP validates it.

steps.action: Check the referenced receiver object and exact error details
steps.reason: You need the object number and business context before validating the master data.

steps.action: Validate the cost center if the item is assigned to cost center
steps.reason: Cost center postings fail when the object is expired, blocked, or in the wrong controlling area.

steps.action: Validate the order or WBS if a different receiver is used
steps.reason: Internal orders and project objects can also be closed, invalid, or blocked for posting.

steps.action: Correct the assignment in the document if appropriate and retest
steps.reason: Once the receiver object is valid, the document should save or post correctly.

title: Investigate a Number Range Problem
description: How to diagnose why SAP cannot assign a document or object number because the interval is missing, invalid, or exhausted.

steps.action: Capture the exact number range error and identify the affected object
steps.reason: Different objects use different number range transactions and context rules.

steps.action: Check the relevant number range object and interval settings
steps.reason: This confirms whether the object and interval exist and whether the correct interval is assigned.

steps.action: Verify fiscal year, company code, and document type dependencies if applicable
steps.reason: Many number ranges depend on year or organizational context, not just the object itself.

steps.action: Create or extend the interval only with proper business approval
steps.reason: Number range maintenance is sensitive and should follow governance rules.

steps.action: Retest the original transaction after the interval issue is resolved
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms numbering now works again.

title: Investigate Missing ALE Change Pointers
description: How to diagnose why expected outbound messages were not generated from business changes because change pointers were not created.

steps.action: Confirm which business change should have triggered an outbound message
steps.reason: You need to verify that the process should actually rely on change pointers.

steps.action: Check whether global change pointer creation is active
steps.reason: If global activation is off, no message type can create change pointers.

steps.action: Check whether the relevant message type is enabled
steps.reason: Even with global activation, the message type itself must also be activated.

steps.action: Review whether change pointer records exist for the object
steps.reason: This confirms whether the problem is missing creation or later processing.

steps.action: Generate or reprocess outbound messages after correcting activation
steps.reason: Once setup is correct, you can trigger the IDoc creation step again.

title: Investigate a Missing or Incorrect Partner Profile
description: How to diagnose IDoc or ALE issues caused by missing or incomplete WE20 partner profile configuration.

steps.action: Identify the sender, receiver, direction, and message type involved
steps.reason: Partner profile diagnosis always starts with the exact communication context.

steps.action: Check whether the partner profile exists for the required partner
steps.reason: A missing partner profile prevents message processing entirely.

steps.action: Review inbound or outbound parameters for the message type
steps.reason: The profile may exist but still be wrong if parameters, process code, or message type setup are incomplete.

steps.action: Validate the related port configuration
steps.reason: Partner profile setup depends on a correct technical port definition.

steps.action: Retest or reprocess the affected message after correction
steps.reason: A final retest confirms the corrected profile now supports the expected processing.

title: Investigate a Failed BDC Session
description: How to diagnose and reprocess a classic batch input session that failed in SM35.

steps.action: Open the failed session and review the processing log
steps.reason: The log usually shows the exact screen or field where the batch input stopped.

steps.action: Determine whether the failure is due to data, screen sequence, or authorization
steps.reason: The corrective action depends on why the BDC recording no longer fits the actual transaction flow.

steps.action: Check authorization failure if the log suggests access problems
steps.reason: Batch input can fail silently on restricted screens or actions.

steps.action: Compare the current transaction flow with the original recording if needed
steps.reason: System changes or new mandatory fields often break old BDC logic.

steps.action: Reprocess the session after correcting the root cause
steps.reason: A successful reprocessing confirms the session can now complete.

title: Investigate an Incorrect Report Variant
description: How to diagnose wrong report output caused by a bad, outdated, or dynamic variant problem.

steps.action: Identify the report and variant actually used
steps.reason: The issue is often that users or jobs are not using the variant you think they are.

steps.action: Display the variant values and compare them with expected business scope
steps.reason: Wrong company codes, dates, plants, or filters are common causes of incorrect results.

steps.action: Check referenced dynamic variables if the variant uses them
steps.reason: A variant can look correct but still behave wrongly because the dynamic variable values are outdated.

steps.action: Check the background job if the issue happens in scheduled execution
steps.reason: Jobs may use a different variant or an outdated saved variant assignment.

steps.action: Correct and save the variant, then rerun the report
steps.reason: A rerun confirms that the updated selection logic now produces the expected output.

title: Investigate a Missing Commit After BAPI Processing
description: How to diagnose why a custom program or integration reports success from a BAPI but no data is actually saved.

steps.action: Confirm the BAPI call returns success or a document number
steps.reason: This distinguishes commit-handling issues from hard validation failures.

steps.action: Review the calling logic or test sequence around the BAPI
steps.reason: Many BAPIs require an explicit commit after the business call.

steps.action: Check whether COMMIT WORK or BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT is executed
steps.reason: Without a proper commit, the update request may never be finalized.

steps.action: Review update task results if the BAPI should have saved data
steps.reason: Sometimes the commit happened but the update failed later.

steps.action: Correct the transaction handling and retest
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms the BAPI now commits data properly.

title: Investigate an Electronic Bank Statement Issue
description: How to diagnose uploaded bank statements that did not post correctly or left items unresolved.

steps.action: Confirm the statement file was uploaded and processed
steps.reason: You first need to verify whether the issue is import failure or posting interpretation failure.

steps.action: Review items that require manual clarification
steps.reason: FEBAN is the key transaction for unresolved statement items and reprocessing.

steps.action: Check account symbols, interpretation rules, and external transaction mapping
steps.reason: Most EBS issues come from interpretation logic and missing posting rules.

steps.action: Correct the posting or mapping setup as needed
steps.reason: You must fix the root cause before reprocessing the unresolved items.

steps.action: Reprocess the bank statement items and verify the postings
steps.reason: A successful reprocessing confirms the statement can now be interpreted correctly.

title: Investigate Missing Foreign Trade or Compliance Data
description: How to diagnose missing customs, trade, or compliance-related data that blocks a logistics process.

steps.action: Identify which document and item triggered the foreign trade or compliance error
steps.reason: You need the exact transaction context before checking master data.

steps.action: Check material master data for the required trade-relevant fields
steps.reason: Commodity code, country, and other compliance fields are often maintained in the material master.

steps.action: Review the sales or delivery document context
steps.reason: Destination country, customer, and shipping data can influence the required trade data.

steps.action: Determine whether the missing data belongs in material, customer, vendor, or document fields
steps.reason: Different foreign trade requirements come from different master data sources.

steps.action: Maintain the missing data and retest the blocked process
steps.reason: The process should only move forward once the required compliance data is complete.

title: Investigate a Service Entry Sheet That Cannot Be Accepted
description: How to diagnose why a service entry sheet is blocked, inconsistent, or not accepted.

steps.action: Open the service entry sheet and review its status and error context
steps.reason: This confirms whether the issue is acceptance, release, quantity, or account assignment related.

steps.action: Compare the service entry sheet with the related purchase order and limits
steps.reason: The SES must align with PO service lines, values, and acceptance rules.

steps.action: Check release requirement, account assignment, and authorization context
steps.reason: Acceptance can fail even when quantities look correct if workflow or authorization is incomplete.

steps.action: Correct the inconsistency and retry acceptance
steps.reason: Once the business inconsistency is fixed, acceptance should proceed normally.

steps.action: Validate downstream invoice or history impact after acceptance
steps.reason: A final check confirms the accepted SES is now available for later procurement processing.

title: Investigate a Missing or Invalid Work Center
description: How to diagnose routing, order, or planning issues caused by work center master data problems.

steps.action: Display the work center and confirm it exists in the correct plant context
steps.reason: The first check is whether the referenced work center is actually present and active.

steps.action: Review validity dates, status, and usage-related master data
steps.reason: A work center can exist but still be unusable because of validity or setup issues.

steps.action: Check capacity and cost center assignments
steps.reason: Incomplete work center setup often affects scheduling, costing, and order processing.

steps.action: Review the routing or task list that references the work center
steps.reason: The issue may be in the task list reference rather than the work center record itself.

steps.action: Correct the work center or routing assignment and retest
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms the production object can now use the work center correctly.

title: Investigate Missing Routing or Task List
description: How to diagnose production or planning failures caused by missing routings, task lists, or production version setup.

steps.action: Identify the material, plant, and process where routing lookup failed
steps.reason: The required routing depends on plant, usage, and validity context.

steps.action: Check whether a valid routing exists for the material and plant
steps.reason: This confirms whether the issue is absence of routing or wrong determination.

steps.action: Review the production version if one is required
steps.reason: Production versions often control which BOM and routing SAP is allowed to use.

steps.action: Check material master production-relevant data
steps.reason: Incorrect plant or production settings can prevent correct routing usage.

steps.action: Retest order creation or planning after the routing setup is corrected
steps.reason: A valid retest confirms SAP can now determine the correct task list.

title: Investigate Missing Production Order Confirmation
description: How to diagnose why a production order or operation has not been confirmed as expected.

steps.action: Review order and operation status to identify missing confirmations
steps.reason: This confirms which operation or phase is still open or inconsistent.

steps.action: Check whether a confirmation exists and whether it posted correctly
steps.reason: The issue may be absence of confirmation or a confirmation that failed downstream.

steps.action: Enter or correct the confirmation if business rules allow it
steps.reason: CO11N is the standard transaction for manual production confirmations.

steps.action: Review whether goods receipt, backflush, or final confirmation dependencies exist
steps.reason: Confirmation often interacts with stock and status updates beyond the order itself.

steps.action: Recheck order status after the confirmation
steps.reason: The final validation confirms the order now reflects the expected progress.

title: Investigate a Missing or Invalid Settlement Rule
description: How to diagnose why an order, WBS, or other cost object cannot be settled.

steps.action: Capture the exact settlement object and error message
steps.reason: Different objects use different settlement logic, so you need the exact failing context first.

steps.action: Check whether a settlement rule exists for the object
steps.reason: Missing settlement rules are a common reason settlement cannot proceed.

steps.action: Review receiver object, validity dates, and distribution percentages
steps.reason: A rule may exist but still be invalid because the receiver or percentages are wrong.

steps.action: Correct the settlement rule in the appropriate maintenance transaction
steps.reason: The rule must be valid before any settlement rerun will work.

steps.action: Repeat settlement after the correction
steps.reason: A successful rerun confirms the cost object can now settle correctly.

title: Investigate an Invalid or Blocked Cost Center
description: How to diagnose posting problems caused by cost center validity, status, or controlling-area issues.

steps.action: Display the cost center and review validity dates
steps.reason: A cost center that is expired for the posting date will fail even if it exists.

steps.action: Check whether the cost center is blocked or in the wrong controlling area
steps.reason: Status or controlling context issues often explain why postings fail unexpectedly.

steps.action: Compare the posting date and organizational context with the cost center master data
steps.reason: A valid cost center in one context can still be invalid in another.

steps.action: Correct master data only if authorized and appropriate
steps.reason: Master data corrections should follow the owning business process.

steps.action: Retry the posting with the corrected or valid cost center
steps.reason: A retest confirms whether the cost center is now acceptable for posting.

title: Investigate a Missing or Incomplete Asset Master
description: How to diagnose failures caused by missing, invalid, or incomplete asset master data.

steps.action: Check whether the asset exists in the expected company code
steps.reason: The simplest failure is often that the referenced asset number is wrong or does not exist.

steps.action: Review class, capitalization context, and depreciation setup
steps.reason: Asset master completeness is essential for downstream postings and periodic processing.

steps.action: Correct incomplete asset master data if the asset exists
steps.reason: Missing master data can often be fixed directly in the existing asset.

steps.action: Create the asset if it is missing and business rules require a new one
steps.reason: If the asset does not exist, you need a valid master record before posting.

steps.action: Retest the asset-related process afterwards
steps.reason: The final check confirms that the posting or process now recognizes the asset properly.

title: Investigate a Failed Asset Accounting Posting
description: How to diagnose an asset posting that fails because of transaction type, depreciation area, or master/config issues.

steps.action: Capture the exact asset accounting error and posting scenario
steps.reason: Asset posting issues vary widely depending on acquisition, transfer, retirement, or manual value adjustment.

steps.action: Review the asset master and depreciation areas
steps.reason: Asset master setup is a common root cause of failed FI-AA postings.

steps.action: Check transaction type and account determination relevance
steps.reason: The posting may fail because the transaction type or account mapping is incomplete.

steps.action: Validate period and value date context
steps.reason: Even a correctly configured asset can reject a posting if the timing is invalid.

steps.action: Repeat the posting after the correction
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms the FI-AA posting setup is now valid.

title: Investigate Missing Depreciation Posting
description: How to diagnose why planned depreciation exists but was not posted or updated as expected.

steps.action: Review the asset values and planned depreciation
steps.reason: You first need to confirm whether the asset actually carries planned depreciation for the relevant period.

steps.action: Check whether periodic depreciation posting has already run
steps.reason: The issue may simply be that the run was never executed for the relevant period.

steps.action: Review background job status if depreciation runs automatically
steps.reason: Automated depreciation runs often fail in background rather than interactively.

steps.action: Correct any asset master or period-related issue
steps.reason: Depreciation gaps usually come from setup, timing, or master data problems.

steps.action: Rerun depreciation posting and verify the result
steps.reason: A successful rerun confirms the expected depreciation is now posted.

title: Investigate a Dunning Run That Missed Items
description: How to diagnose why overdue customer receivables were not selected in a dunning run.

steps.action: Review whether the relevant customer items are truly open and overdue
steps.reason: Dunning cannot pick up items that are cleared, not due, or otherwise excluded.

steps.action: Check customer dunning data in master data
steps.reason: Missing or incorrect dunning procedures or blocks in master data can exclude the account.

steps.action: Check document-level dunning block or special status
steps.reason: The block may exist on the invoice even if customer master data looks fine.

steps.action: Review the dunning run parameters and selection scope
steps.reason: Wrong run date, company code, or procedure settings can exclude expected items.

steps.action: Repeat the dunning proposal after correcting the issue
steps.reason: A successful proposal confirms the items are now eligible.

title: Investigate a Tax Code Problem
description: How to diagnose posting failures caused by missing, invalid, or misconfigured tax codes.

steps.action: Capture the exact tax-related error and transaction context
steps.reason: Tax issues depend on whether the posting is FI, MM, or SD related.

steps.action: Check whether the tax code exists and is maintained correctly
steps.reason: Missing or incomplete tax code configuration is a common root cause.

steps.action: Verify the tax code is valid for the company code and business scenario
steps.reason: A valid tax code in one scenario may still be wrong for another transaction type or country setup.

steps.action: Review tax account and procedure setup if the code exists but still fails
steps.reason: The issue may be in the downstream tax configuration, not the code itself.

steps.action: Retest the posting after correcting the tax setup
steps.reason: A successful retest confirms the tax code is now valid and usable.

title: Investigate an Open Item Clearing Problem
description: How to diagnose why customer or vendor items cannot be cleared together.

steps.action: Check the open customer items if the issue is in AR
steps.reason: You need to confirm the items are still open and identify their posting details.

steps.action: Check the open vendor items if the issue is in AP
steps.reason: For vendor clearing, FBL1N gives the equivalent open-item visibility.

steps.action: Compare currency, amount, assignment, and business context of the items
steps.reason: Mismatch in basic clearing criteria often explains why the items do not clear as expected.

steps.action: Determine whether residual item, partial payment, or write-off handling is required
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is not an error, but that a different clearing method is needed.

steps.action: Retry the clearing using the correct method
steps.reason: A successful retry confirms the correct clearing scenario was applied.

title: Investigate House Bank Determination Problems
description: How to diagnose why a payment run cannot determine a valid house bank or house bank account.

steps.action: Review the payment method, company code, and bank determination context
steps.reason: House bank selection depends on the exact payment run setup and business context.

steps.action: Check bank determination and ranking order settings
steps.reason: This is the core customizing area for how payment methods choose bank accounts.

steps.action: Verify that the house bank and account are maintained correctly
steps.reason: Missing account-level setup can prevent payment medium creation and payment selection.

steps.action: Confirm the payment method and currency align with the bank setup
steps.reason: A valid house bank in general may still be unusable for the specific payment context.
